title: vimeo_1110834968
description: Wednesday, 18 March 2026 | Environment Committee Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill Waikato Regional Council Te Tōpuni Kōkōrangi Liana Poutu, Chair Sean Zieltjes, Planner Nga Hapu o Ngati Porou Management Arrangements Horiana Irwin-Easthope, Project Manager Tasman District Council One NZ, Fortysouth, Connexa, Spark Graeme McCarrison Colin Clune Fiona Matthews Gisborne District Council Rehette Stoltz, Mayor Te Runanga o Ngati Kuia Trust Eugene Whakahoehoe, Tumu Toihau Te Rūnanga o Ngāti Kuia Raymond Smith, Te Ihu Kaikowāho Marlborough District Council Nadine Taylor, Mayor Pere Hawes, Head of Environmental Policy Kim Lawson, Solicitor/Strategic Planner Lighting Council New Zealand Bryan King, Executive Director Dark Skies Group, Royal Astronomical Society of New Zealand Independent Electricity Generators Association Inc Te Kahui Maru Trust Auckland Airport
descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. Waikato Regional Council

chunks.text: Grant Kell on the, I'm chairing of this subcommittee today. Uh, we've got fifteen minutes of your time. We have your submission. Please take it as read, but feel free to highlight a few of the key points, and we'd love a few ques- opportunity for some questions. So the floor is yours. Yeah. So, good morning, everybody, and thank you for the, opportunity to speak to you in person today, on the Natural, Environment and Planning Bills.

Uh, Warima, chair of Waikato Regional Council. So good morning. Um, so the Waikato Regional Council overall supports the intention to replace the RMA and to simplify the resource management system. Uh, but it's something we need to get right. So we need to have resource management legislation that is clear, workable, and will withstand election cycles.

So that is a key for us. Um, I won't go over what you've probably heard from others, but just want to focus in on what's important for the Waikato region. Um, so Waikato region, we are twenty percent of the land area of the North Island. Ten percent approximately of New Zealand's, population live and work in our region. We are ten percent approximately of the New Zealand GDP, which makes us the fourth largest economically producing region. Uh, we have fifteen percent of the country's councils.

Uh, so that's twelve local government entities, either in the, the region completely or a part of. Um, big part of our region is our renewable energy resources. Uh, the majority of Taupō's volcanic geothermal area is, is in our region, as well as items like the Lake Taupō, Waikato River, Hauraki Plains, significant wetlands, coastal areas, including the Firth of Thames and Coromandel, which are obviously part of the Hauraki Gulf Park.

Uh, we have forty iwi and, a hundred and eighty hapū in our region. So we have fifteen years of co-governance, co-management with our river iwi. Excuse me. Uh, and it's a place that we are comfortable in, and we're national leaders in this field. So we wish to see those deep relationships and foundation of trust that we have built with iwi, and have been the cornerstone of previous resource management system, to go forward and actually strengthen in this new system. We acknowledge that our sys- our, submission is lengthy, and we hope that it has been viewed constructively.

So the main themes of our submissions are highlighted on paragraph eight on pages two and three, and they're also further elaborated on pages four through to fourteen with accompanying detail. Uh, we have a couple of key matters that we would like to draw to the subcommittee's attention. Um, and one for me before I pass over to Chris McClay, our CE, is drawing on my recent experiences sitting as a commissioner on our coastal plan hearing. Uh, having the likes of Aquaculture New Zealand, seafood industry, Royal Forest and Bird, and New Zealand Sports Fishing Council being

able to participate, in the process has added immense value to that process. Our submission on paragraph sixty-one and submission point one zero four asks that the restriction to qualifying residents, is kept in the consent process, but removed from plan making, and that enables those sector and interest groups to participate in that plan making process. Uh, I'll now hand over to Chris, with some more key matters for us. Thank you.

Yeah. Uh, thank you through the chair, Grant and, and committee members for this opportunity. I've got two specialists with me, Tracy May and Mark Brocklesby, in case there's any questions o-of a technical nature. I'm gonna focus on four matters which are of importance to the Waikato. Um, the first of these is the proposed permitted activity system. I'd refer to paragraph sixty-five on page thirteen of our submission, submission point seventy-six on page eighteen, and submission point one forty-four on page forty-eight. We do see some fish hooks in the new permitted

activity system that will make things prac-- challenging from a practical point of view, particularly if we're trying to reduce red tape. The provisions in effect make a pseudo consent process in our mind that adds a lot of impost to what should be a simple system. Presently drafted, the proposed permitted activity system strongly resembles a consent process with registration and monitoring requirements. Our council has had one of the most permissive regional plans in operation in the country for the last twenty years or

so. We have focused on proactive monitoring of high-risk activities rather than monitoring all activities or requiring all activities to register. Our suggested amendments allow for more discretion, hoping to make things simpler to manage activities without additional system cost to both authorities and landowners. In other words, we think our amendment-- our, our suggestions would make things simpler, faster, and cheaper than, than is in the legislation.

The next matter I'd like to raise is to do with, Te Tiriti o Waitanga or Te Awa o Waikato, which is more commonly known as the vision and strategy for the Waikato and Waipā rivers, and specifically the equivalent effect, that is, introduced into the legislation. I referred in our submission to paragraph E on page four, paragraph twenty-eight on page nine, submi-submission point eighty-three on page twenty, submission point one zero two on page twenty-nine, and submission point one seventy-one on page sixty-four.

Simply, Te Tiriti o Waitanga, the vision and strategy, is the primary direction-setting document for the Waikato River and activities within its catchment. Under iwi river settlement legislation, where there's inconsistency between Te Tiriti o Waitanga and any national direction, then Te Tiriti o Waitanga prevails. Now, this is absolutely central to understandUnder the Treaty Settlement legislation, if there's an inconsistency between Te Tiriti o Waitangi and any proposed national or any national direction, then Te Tiriti o Waitangi prevails.

It was inserted directly into our-- into the Waikato Regional Policy Statement, by the government without the requirement to progress through a Schedule One plan change process. So it's imperative that the new legislation is crystal clear how the existing Treaty settlement obligations in relation to Te Tiriti o Waitangi will be met. Clause nine, subclause one of the Natural Environment Bill references the equivalent effect where the Crown will work with post-settlement governance entities to seek agreement on how Treaty settlement

redress will operate with equivalency. We recommend including recognition of Te Tiriti o Waitangi in both bills to ensure its status is upheld in the new resource management system, and we seek that Te Tiriti o Waitangi is deemed to be part of the Regional Spatial Plan, the Natural Environment Plan, and also the land use plans for the Waikato region.

The next point I'd like to raise is to do with the Lake Taupō nutrient management framework. This is referred to on page eleven-- sorry, paragraph eleven, page seven, submission, submission point one sixty-seven on page sixty-two, and submission point one sixty-nine on page sixty-four. Taxpayers and ratepayers have invested eighty-one billion dollars of public money to improve water quality in the Lake Taupō catchment.

In addition, local government and landowners have incurred significant additional costs. The nitrogen cap and trade system that was introduced was the first of its kind in the world. It has successfully resulted in a reduction of nitrogen entering the lake by purchasing at least twenty percent of the nitrogen that was put onto land, or around a hundred and seventy tons per, per hectare that was lost into the lake system.

And that was done using the eighty-one billion dollars of public money. Simply, we cannot risk having this approach undone inadvertently by the new system or a transition to the new system. Careful consideration of the implications of the bills on this existing nutrient capping planning framework is needed, including ensuring that nationally prescribed approaches do not undermine or limit the ability to continue to operate established local catchment-specific arrangements. Well-defined environment limits exist for the catchment, and that are maintained through the use of these

innovative economic instruments, exactly what the system reform is seeking to champion. So we ask that the new system provides a mechanism for the current provisions to effectively be lifted and shifted easily into the new legislation and given effect to in the Natural Environment Plan. The final point I'd like to raise is of a more technical matter, and it's to do with clauses thirty-two and one sixty-four of the Natural Environment Bill or section seventy and one oh seven, which are in the Resource Management Act, which have been taken over into

those new clauses. This relates to matters that have been brought to our attention through the recent Environment Court process for a significant plan change for the Waikato for land use. That is Plan Change One. The court paused making a decision while the Resource Management Act recently was amended.

So to do with clause thirty-two or section seventy, and it relates to submission point one forty-one on page forty-six of our submission. So recently, the government amended the Resource Management Act at our request to enable permitted activities under section seventy. That is where some environmental limits, cannot be met. This was essential for the Environment Court to consider the validity of giving permitted activity status for some low-risk farming activities in Plan Change One. Without that change, an additional two thousand eight hundred farmers would need

resource consent, probably for little benefit if any. Our submission simply recommends that clause thirty-two, the principles for classifying activities of the Natural Environment Bill, which is equivalent to section seventy of the RMA, should reflect the amendments that were made to the section via the Consenting and Other System Changes Amendment Act in August of last year. Curiously, presently they do not. As drafted, clause thirty-two reverts back to the previous version of section seventy of the RMA.

This means that councils would require resource consents, from landowners for activities where there's an environment, environmental limit already being breached. Um, and that would be for either permit-permitted activities or consents. So if left as drafted, rules requiring some consents or permits, I should say, in the new system, would need to be written for farming activities that are presently permitted activities in our regional plan. Now, looking at the companion provision for the granting of those permits and consents.

So this is clause one sixty-four of the new bill or section one oh seven of the current RMA, and it's referred to in submission point two ten, on page seventy-nine. While we support clause one sixty-four, matters for which permit must not be granted, we, we suggest that this clause should also consider that where there's already a breach of a limit, the consenting authority does have the ability to make a decision on an application.Currently, Clause 164 states that where an environment limit is breached, the consenting authority is unable to

grant a permit or consent. Our suggested amendment to Clause 164C on page 79 provides for a consenting or s- a permitting pathway with the necessary considerations. As stated in our submission point, we consider that a permitting authority should be able to grant a permit if another approach similar to RMA Section 1072A is adopted. Our sub- suggested amendments at submission point 141 on page 46 and submission point 210 on page 79 would address these matters. Uh, finding it's quite a technical matter, but just in an attempt to, to

simplify the, the conundrum I've just described, I'll just describe it like this. As presently written, when an activity is unable to be considered as a permitted activity, as written in Clause 32, then a consent's required. But where a consent is required, one not, cannot be granted because of the way that Clause 164 is written.

Something doesn't quite add up to us, and we believe it needs to be corrected. The, it will have a significant-- this will have a significant impact on activities undertaken in our region, and as previously mentioned, at least 2,800 low-risk farming activities would need to obtain, permits, which we cannot grant.

Our amendments seek for this, to, to, provide a c- a pathway in both instances. Pathways that allow the options of assessing the current breach of the environment limit and so that everybody can be satisfied that conditions can be applied that will contribute to remedying the environment limit breach over time. Th- th- th- Pass that to Ryan to finish.

Uh, well, I'm, I'm happy not to wind up right now. Um, I see Ryan's got his hand up for questions, and I'd rather, I'd rather w-we got the questions. Yeah. That'd be great. So I think that's all right. Um, and thank you. Very good submission. Thanks. Some great points. I'll let the, the member of Parliament for the fastest growing city in the country have a say. Thank, thanks, Chair. Hey, thanks, team, for your presentation.

And, yeah, obviously a lot of similar themes coming in from all over the country. Um, couple of quick questions for you guys. In your submission- Mm-hmm... you talk about, data sets and, you mention significant, gaps in environmental data are needed to set limits. So I guess my question is what, what data sets or monitoring capabilities are most lacking, and what would it, what would it help to sort of fill that for you guys from a, compliance or a, a council perspective?

You want that, Christine? Yeah. Um, thanks, Ryan. I guess for us, the, some of those data sets are really just consistency of data across the entire region. One that springs to mind is probably groundwater. That's an area of, focus or for gaps, identifying gaps. And also, as you'll be aware, a third of our region is in fact the coastal marine area, and, and it's a bit of a poor cousin to, some of the data that we have on land and freshwater.

Okay. Thanks for that. And one more question if I could, Chair. Yep, fire away. Last one. Yep. Um, where'd it go? Oh, just on gaps between the two bills. You've identified gaps, and you'd like us to consider effects on wetlands, geothermal features, outstanding natural landscapes. I understand some of this might come through the policy direction, in next, next statements, but were there any other conflicting or gaps you think we need to be aware of between the two bills?

Um, me again, Ryan. Um, I think the, the key is making sure under the planning bill where we are address, addressing kind of land use activities, that we are also making sure some of the environmental limits are taken account of. Um, and one that springs to mind, particularly with respect to residential and commercial activity, is freshwater and that link to water allocation and, water quantity.

Yeah. Okay. Thank you. Uh, look, thank you very much. We've reached our, allotted time. Really appreciate your submission. Very, very helpful. And, thank you very much. Have a great day. Well, hey, thanks, Ryan. And, and more than happy to make any of our staff available to, your officials, that are advising you in the select committee.

Yeah. The, the technical points you pointed out, they're very helpful. Thank you very much. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you. All the best. Okay. Cheers. Right. We should have our next, Te Topuni Kokorangi. Are they up next? Who... Here we go. Is that Sean I see there?

Hi. Kia ora. Good morning. Is it on? Uh, tena koutou. Um- Yeah. Good morning. Uh, hi. Good morning. Uh, Grant McMillan, I'm the chair of the subcommittee. Uh, we have 15 minutes with you. Um, we, you take your submission as read, but please feel free to highlight a few key points, and away we go. Thank you.

Welcome. Right. E oku rangatira, tena koutou katoa. Uh, ko Liana Poutu toku ingoa. Ko au te, pou whakarae, te hiamana me kī o Te Topuni Kokorangi. Kei konei, toku ringaringa, Sean Zilches. Uh, e mihi ana ki a koutou tena ata. Uh, good morning, everybody. My name is Liana Poutu.

Uh, I, along with Sean Zilches, are here presenting on behalf of Te Kāhui Tupua, which is the legal personality for Taranaki Maunga and the other peaks. Uh, the entity that we represent, is Te Topuni Kokorangi, so I chair that entity. Um, and also, Sean is our technical planner for Te Topuni Kokorangi. Uh, as you said, we're not gonna read through our submission. We will, will highlight a few things. I guess the, the first thing I want to, um-Probably kick off with is in our settlement legislation, Te Türe Whakatupua mo te Kahui

Tupua. Uh, Te Tupane Kookorangi has legal standing to participate in a range of, of fora, to represent Te Kahui Tupua. One of our, our key concerns, I guess the concern that underlies, our position on, on these bills is that we have, legal standing to participate. Uh, that's-- that is also extended outside of the boundaries of the national park. So although the legal personality, is described as being the legal personality for the peaks, we also have standing to, operate

in fora where the impacts are, are greater than, than the ring plane, what we refer to as the ring plane of, of the national park of Te Papakura o Taranaki. One of the, the concerns we have with the bill is the way that Te Kahui Tupua as a legal personality has been included in the, the broad definition of, of Maori land, interested Maori land.

And, and one of the reasons for that is because it doesn't fully, prescribe and define what the extent of Te Kahui Tupua is. So I don't think we're even named currently in the bill. Uh, there's a reference there to Te Urwera, which is one of the other legal personalities, over a natural feature. And so that definition really, contains and seems to restrict, what our settlement legislation actually provides for Te Kahui Tupua and us as the representative face and, voice entity. So I guess it's, it's with that that our,

I guess our submissions are really, are based around the fact that these bills aim to or-- they, they seem to restrict, what we already have in our settlement legislation. So I'm gonna hand now to Sean, who's gonna talk through, really just highlight for you some of those technical issues, and then, and then I'll close us off at the end. So kia ora, Sean.

Tena tatou. Uh, yeah, as Leanne has referred to the, I guess, the reference to Te Kahui Tupua is relatively clumsy in the bills at the moment. It, it's shoehorned in to identify Maori land, and then the provisions of both bills that hang from that definition, dramatically constrain and I guess look to redefine what Te Kahui Tupua is, which, which rubs up against, Te Tiriti o Kupe Takirauano and, and the legislation that implements that. Um, that has quite a significant practical effect for Te Kahui Tupua, both in terms of how

it, can implement its standing in resource management matters, and then secondly, how, how difficult it, how difficult it will be to implement some of those within the transition time frames that are, that are set in the legislation. There's a real challenge of, trying to renegotiate or re- recast some of these arra- some of the settlement arrangements or the arrangements for Te Kahui Tupua in a two-year window concurrent to national planning frameworks, regional spatial plans, local area plans all starting to, to, to be developed and hit the

road. And for a legal person such as Te Kahui Tupua, which sits broadly across the Taranaki region, that, that's quite a fundamental and substantial impact that, that as, as Leanne has mentioned, starts to rub up against what the, what Te Tiriti o Kupe Takirauano, sets out. Um, the, the, the redress and, and Te Kahui Tupua have a set of values, Ngā Pu Vakatupua, and Te Kahui Tupua status, which, which, to be, have, have, the ability to be considered and, and influence any activity that may impact on Te Kahui Tupua. So I think the-- I guess the rub is

pretty, pretty evident in that when the scope of planning, plan making, the scope of any resource consent or equivalent type, processes is, is narrowed dramatically, even more so when it's shoehorned into the definition of identified Maori land. Um, look, turning, turning to, I guess, the plan making processes that, that sit in both bills, there's no standing for Te Kahui Tupua. Um, it's another discretionary interest to be weighed.

It would need to submit alongside everybody else, to, to, to, I guess, to provide that advice and provide that input into any plan making, which starts to push up against the, the, the express, provisions of the, the settlement legislation fifty-three two. Um, so look, in our submission, we've provided some, some drafting.

It's-- She's pretty fast drafting. So I think that, it's more the, it's more the principles and ideas than that drafting more so than, than the wording specifically that we're wedded to. But those really just seek to, insert into the process the, the, the recognition of standing and the role of Ngāpu Vakatupua and Te Kahui Tupua within, within, within the Taranaki region. But I'll, I'll s- I'll stop there 'cause that's sort of the fundamental issues. There's a range of other kind of bits that flow from that and, and the intricacies of, of both bills, but, but the-- it

all starts from, I guess, the way this-- these bills are cast and the way that they, um- The way they understand Te Kāhui Tupua. Right. So sections nine and ten of, the Natural Environments Bill as it is are what we're referring to as the equivalency, equivalency bills, pa- provisions, sorry.

And so what, what those do for us is say that while there is a negotiation to be had between the pa- between the Crown and treaty settlement partners, to determine how best to recognize and uphold treaty settlements, that to the greatest extent possible, that will be, you know, the existing, the existing arrangements will be, applied. Uh, and certainly for that, that two-year period that's prescribed. Our view is it took us nine years to negotiate these arrangements, and I'm not hopeful that a two-year, period in which to

resolve a number of, of issues is appropriate. And we were one-- we were only one treaty, treaty redress, arr- set of arrangements. I think the, the other point is greatest extent possible gives us less than what we currently have legislatively. And so there are things that will need to be resolved. I don't believe that, two years will be enough. And once that, I think it's nine three that repeals the section after two years from a- from, enforcement date, we're sort of left in a, in a situation where the

Crown can't usu- can't unilaterally amend treaty settlements without the agreement of the treaty partner. Uh, and so we run ourselves into a point where actually we're going to be kind of deadlocked potentially because we just don't have and haven't had the time to work through what these arrangements mean going forward. So I think we'll end it there so that we can, enable questions from the committee. Tēnā koutou.

Thank you very much. Um, Huhana, I believe, has a question for you. Tēnā koe, Leana. O tira, tēnā koe, Shaun, mo tō wāhi kōrero i tēnei ata. Um, wanting to really talk with you around, engagement and, just the way that our Kāhui Tupua has been treated, in the preparation and drafting of this legislation.

We've heard that it's not even listed within the bills. Um, was there any engagement from Crown officials with you, in preparation? And what was the quality of that engagement if you were i- included in conversations? So no, no engagement specifically with Te Kāhui Tupua, with Te Tupahi Kōkōrangi. Uh, in fairness, we are relatively new entity. Our, our settlement legislation went through at the beginning of last year. Um, however, we've been in negotiation for nine years, so, you know, I don't use

it. That's not an excuse for not knowing that these arrangements were, were coming. So it's-- we've had none. We've had no engagement, no input. Uh, and, and I believe that this has been done back to front. I thought you would resolve, how treaty settlements will move forward in the new planning space prior to introducing bills that have holding clauses essentially to say, "We're gonna give you two years to renegotiate and re-agree every treaty settlement that's, you know, occurred in the country." I, I think that's unrealistic,

yeah, and, and, and unfair on the parties to have to do that in that timeframe. Mr. Chair, a supplementary on that one. Yeah. Yeah, go on. So, you know, you, you, you are a known entity. Uh, y- the, the settlement came through the house. We were there and, provided speeches of support.

So you weren't included or even considered. We, we see that in the legislation. Uh, and yet, this approach was meant to be a new way of environmental governance, of that mahi tahi, in looking after, you know, the kāhui maunga as we have with the Ngāhere and Niuerevera and Te Awatapua.

So, i- in my mind, why should you need to relitigate a treaty settlement that the ink has barely dried, for this legislation? Because really you're a whakauruho, and, and what has been agreed should be upheld by any government, in environmental reform.

Because really you're already providing the framework as to how the mahi tahi is meant to occur. Is that correct? Agree one hundred percent. Agree one hundred percent. And, and in fact, you know, Taranaki Maunga and Te Papakura o Taranaki, the national park, provides all of the sustenance to our region.

It provides all of the you know, the water to our region. Um, it's a key component to, to how, environmental, planning, you know, done in this region. Taranaki Maunga is a key component, and at present, Taranaki Maunga is excluded from having a role in helping develop those.

Kia ora. Yeah. Well, thank you very lot. We've reached our time. So look, thank you very much for your, contribution this morning. Really appreciate it. And, we'll, yeah, we'll-- and, yeah, have a great day. So thank you. Kia ora. Tēnā koutou. Ngā mihi. Right, so we'll now bring up our next submitter.

Next submitter, Ngā Hapū o Ngāti Porou. Here we go. Kia ora. Tena koe. Good morning. Ah, yes. Ah, yes, we, we, we meet again. Good morning. Kia ora. Uh, hi, Grant McCallum here. Um, welcome to the, subcommittee, of the Environment Select Committee this morning.

I'm chairing this one, and we've got-- you've got 15 minutes of our time. Please take your submission as read, but you're welcome to highlight a few points, and then we'll leave time for questions. It's all yours. Thank you. Ka pai, tena tatou. I'll just check you can hear me okay. Perfect. Thank you. Excellent. Um, well, morning everybody. Um, I appear for Ngā Rohe Moana o Ngāti Porou alongside my whanaunga, Matanuku Mahuika, and Agnes Walker, who you can see on screen.

My name's Huriana Uenis Tope. I'm here in a legal capacity. I'm gonna hand it over to Matanuku to start the submission this morning, and then, he'll pass back over to me for some technical points, and then we'll leave time for questions. So I'll hand over to you, Ma. Tena koutou. Um, tena koe, e te hiamona, and members of the committee. Um, I'll be relatively quick. I'm, I'm here, I was the legal advisor through the majority of this process through to 2015 when my father, who was the chief negotiator, passed away, and I became the, the negotiator at that point through to the passing

of the bespoke Ngāti Porou legislation in 2019. Um, the, the context of this is that, this was a, a bespoke agreement that, well, negotiations for which began in the end of 2003, start of 2004, following, was then Labor government's announcing its intention to legislate to prevent, Māori claiming customary title in relation to the foreshore and seabed.

Um, and the, the challenge for us through this negotiation was balancing both the, the politics of the foreshore and seabed, but, but also the-- our view at the time, and it remains our view, was that this was a national response, but it failed to take into account our very specific circumstances.

Um, and we've discussed those circumstances previously. By far the majority population living in the Tairāwhiti is Ngāti Porou. Um, we've retained the vast majority of our coastal lands. We maintain a very, strong and ongoing connection to the, the taku tai. We don't wanna keep the public out, but we do want to make sure that our interests are protected. So the challenge that we had was: How do we, how do we ensure that we, are able to exert, a level of influence which is commensurate with the strength of our connection,

but we do so in a, in a way which navigates around the various, statutory instruments? A-and the context of the RMA, the RMA is different in the marine environment, and it's different because on dry land you can identify a landowner. It deals with environmental impacts but doesn't allocate resources. In the marine environment, it has an allocative ex- effect. It allocates space. So it's necessary to interact with that regime in order to, prevent it from undermining, you know, the rights that we say that we have and that were recognized through this agreement.

So the, the short point is that, you know, we worked our way through, ensuring that we had effective oversight and input, into the management of the taku tai moana which, at the most extreme ends, where we have the strongest interests, we can prevent an activity from occurring, accepting ongoing public access, and also accepting certain things of national significance. So it was a very carefully crafted compromise. An amendment to the RMA as being proposed will affect that compromise a-and will effectively be a, you know, a

repositioning of us a-and will require a ne- a renegotiation of this agreement. Um, I, I won't take up any, any further time and will perhaps pass it over to Huriana. Good, and so just reinforcing the kōrero that you heard from the previous submitter, this is a similar situation and also different. So similar in the sense that, the management arrangements under the bespoke Ngā Hapū o Ngāti Porou, Deed and Act provides the management arrangements with specific legal standing to participate in particular processes that are currently provided for under the RMA.

And as Matanuku's indicated, that occurs across the spectrum. So depending on the rights that are recognized, that will, lend itself to a more greater role for Ngā Rohe Moana o Ngāti Porou and Ngā Hapū o Ngāti Porou in those processes. And so what is happening here is with the reframing and shifting of the system and, in some instances, changing completely, that changes the nature of then how Ngā Hapū can participate in that system.

So what we've tried to do in the submission is be really specific about that. So, I didn't wanna reiterate points that, that Ngā Hapū's support of the submission you heard yesterday from Tirunanga Nui o Ngāti Porou, because there's been a lot of collaboration in this space across Ngāti Porou entities. But at paragraph 30 of our submission, that's where we're really getting into some of kind of what I would say is the nitty-gritty of the, the effects on the arrangements themselves and really highlighting also that, these arrangements are specifically named in the bills, and

they are not treaty settlements. And so getting that also through to, officials who we have been engaging with, can sometimes be a challenge because understanding that difference and therefore the unique lens that you have to apply when you're understanding these arrangements. Look, we-- you know, it can be challenging, but that is the task, that is the obligation on all of us to understand these arrangements that the Crown has entered into and said that they will uphold.

Um, and we're up for that. Um, it's just how you do that. And at the moment, our position is that the bills do not uphold the arrangements, and we've got real concerns about how, the specific-Mechanisms within the act are going to be eroded through this process. So I did want to leave time for questions, but also just signaling that, if you are looking for, okay, what is the rub, part of it's just contained in paragraph 30 there. So, happy to take questions.

May, may I say one more thing, Horiana? Yeah, ka ako ma. Um, I, I think the way to, to look at this is, is that the outcome that the different mechanisms produce rather than the statutory framework through which, they, they would operate, 'cause we're very much focused on the outcome. If you think about the, the, you know, the s- the, the sort of like the statutory acknowledgement, that, that is around the ability for us to know what's happening in that Takutai Moana. The environmental covenant is ensuring that our values are taken

into account when people are making decisions in relation to the Takutai Moana. The permission right is about saying where we have title, there are certain things that we need to agree to because of the nature of the interference, with our entry. So, so think about it in those terms rather than the statutory regime itself. The statutory regime was just the regime that existed, and so therefore that was how it gave it expression.

But we were, we were really thinking about it in terms of what is the right that it gives to participate, and the RMA was simply the vehicle that existed at the time to enable that. And all we're looking to do here is maintain the status quo. We're not trying to get any preference or any special deal out of it. We just wanna make sure that we're unaffected.

Thank you. Great. Thank you. So happy to take questions now? Excellent. Righto. Um, okay. Huhana I believe has a question. Thank you. All right. Uh, tēnā koutou nga hapu o Ngati Porou. O te rai te whaea Agnes. Um, koutou ko Mata Nuku, ko Horiana, e mihi nui ana ki a koutou.

Loud and clear e tatou ma. Loud and clear. Pera i te kāhui maunga, ko koutou anō tērā. Um, from West Coast to East Coast, we are hearing clear, consistent messages of kahore, and want to acknowledge, the work that you have undertaken over many years, to achieve these arrangements. And similar to what I asked of our Taranaki whanaunga, do you believe that the arrangements that you have are like a korowai or a whakaruruhau or that safety blanket for the environment, for the people, for the lands and sea, for Ngā Hapu o Ngāti Porou and the wider

community? And, you know, you need to see these arrangements upheld and honored without being relitigated via legislation. I was waiting for Agnes to see if she would say something, but, um- Uh, look, I mean, we, you could, you could describe it that way. Uh, I mean, these arrangements are about, um... I think of what one of our uncles, the late Eru Paenga, said shortly after the announcement was made, and his comment was, "You know, I've been alive 70-something years," it was at that time, "and this whole time I thought

this piece of coast was mine, and now I'm being told that it isn't." So the, the, the, the objective was always to maintain that, that connection in an enduring way. But I need to be really clear, like we accepted right away public access, public use navigation. We, we, we-- this is not intended to be something which is, which is exclusionary. This is very much about trying to protect, but, but also recognizing, you know, manaaki is a way you can describe it, that, that, you know, we, we exist in a, in a place where everybody has a, has a, a, a wish to, to go to the

coast. And, you know, we don't wish to exclude them, but we do want to ensure that that connection is maintained, and so these agreements are all about that. Thank you. And just one more, Mr. Chair. Yeah. One second, then I'm going to Rachel. Thank you. Thank you. Um, I reflect on the Auditor-General's recent report to the Māori Affairs Select Committee that examined, compliance or the way that, the Crown and its entities uphold, or even plan how they're going to support giving effect to treaty settlements and arrangements such as this.

Um, again, maybe we're not learning fast enough as kāwanatanga as to how to honor these arrangements, and it's more of a mihi and a thank you for you coming before us. So I'll pass the time over to Rachel. Kia rohanamae engari e mihi nui ana. Yeah. Uh, Rachel Brooking has a question. Yeah. Over to you, Rachel. Thank you. And, and hello again. Um, we-- you, you talk about, hapū and iwi involvement in national direction setting and the importance of that. Um, can you describe to us what's changing and how you think would, what would be the best,

possible way for, and most appropriate way for involvement? Do you want me to take that one, Matt? Yes, absolutely. Um, yes, sure. And look, this is something that I think has been causing a lot of angst, particularly in our rohe. Um, and I'll only speak for, for, Ngā Hapu o Ngāti Porou and obviously Te Runanga Nui o Ngāti Porou.

But I mean, Te Tai Rawhiti and the submission is framed in the reality that, you know, our whanaunga live in up the coast, which is one that has, you know, received a lot of national attention lately. But that is the daily reality of our whanaunga that live in places like Te Araroa, Hicks Bay, Tokomaru Bay. Um, and so what we see as part of the impact of more centralization into national direction setting and limiting the ability, for councils to, to really give effect to bespoke arrangements is that we are further away

from having a, an impact on and, input into what is affecting our daily reality. And so what that looks like for Ngāti Porou, and you heard it yesterday as well from Te Runanga Nui, is there is the ability to create bespoke arrangements here, and that is not to run up against what-We think and accept, appropriate national direction mechanisms.

That's not to say we're against national direction, not at all. Um, but there needs to be the ability to develop bespoke arrangements within those considerations. And so that is, probably highlighted more in the Runanga Nuie submission, but also raised in the Ngā Hapu submission. What does that actually look like? That means that we are engaging with those processes early, and it also means that we are given the opportunity to say, "Hey, actually, in Te Tairāwhiti, as the country can see, things are not working, and so we need to be able to design and work

with the Crown, to design processes that are going to ensure that people can live their best lives up there. Um, and those of us who live outside of the rohe that want to move back can do that, in our, in our lifetime." So I think that requires, some more work and some more thinking about what bespoke arrangements can be, provided for in areas like Te Tairāwhiti.

Mm. And, and we have a very good relationship, across the different groups within the Tairāwhiti. We have our moments like all families do. Uh, but, but, but we also have particularly recently a history of, of quite good collaboration where we need to. That, that becomes more challenging when you, when you centralise and you move away.

Um, as Horiana says, we can't speak for anybody else, but we can speak to our own experience, and that's what it's been. Unmute myself is always helpful. Thank you. Look, thank you very much for your submission. We've reached our time, a lot of time this morning.

Um, I really appreciate yours and, please have a great day. Cheers. Thank you very much. Thank you. All right. Tena tātou. Right. We'll now move on to our next submitter, who I believe is Tasman District Council. Welcome back, Clark. I see you there.

Here we go. Tasman District Council, here we go. Morning team. Um, Grant McKelley, I'm chairing the select committee this morning, the subcommittee. Uh, we've got fifteen minutes with your, of your time. Uh, we've got-- Take your, please take your submissions read. Feel free to highlight a few points, but love it if you can leave, some time for questions. It's all up to you. Cheers.

Good as gold. Uh, thanks very much for the opportunity to speak. Um, so I'm Tim King, the Mayor of Tasman. Uh, this is Barry Johnson, so our, Manager of, Planning. So, yep, highlights, that generally we're supportive of the, I guess, the intent of the legislation around simplifying, around streamlining, around making more cost effective. Um, our concern is a-around some of the details and whether or not they actually enable that outcome, or whether potentially, they may work in slight somewhat contrary fashion. I've got a few points to make and then, hopefully there'll be time for

questions. Our first point is, as a unitary authority, we're concerned that there's a bit of a misalignment between the two bills, and for us, that potentially dismantles the integration we currently have, which is kind of contradictory to the local government reform objectives that are being, discussed around the country. Uh, and also prevents us bundling consents in a way that applicants can have one process that covers, all their consent requirements for any particular project or outcome. So that's a really key concern for us, and we think that potentially for unitary authorities, that means

it's a step backwards from what is already a fairly well integrated system and process, to something not quite so. Um, we have concerns about capability and capacity right across, the sector, and that starts at central government with MFE, all the way through the system, regional TAs. You know, just the, the, the number of skilled people across the system that are gonna be needed to implement, the legislation. Uh, so that remains a big issue for us in terms of both cost and complexity, and the financial risk of trying to compete for

resources in order to play our part. We're concerned about the timeframe, particularly the potential for us to be out consulting, and particularly with spatial planning, prior to seeing national direction and limit setting. Uh, and again, the risk that that provides while we're consulting with our community over our part of the process without knowing the certainty that comes through that national direction, and limit setting.

We have concerns around some of the concepts which are, I guess vague by definition. Um, so regulatory relief, and we'll touch more on this in a minute, and reasonable use. Terms that seem likely to generate quite a bit of, potential legal, action over time as we try and sort out what they actually mean in terms of case law, because they're not particularly specific. Uh, while I guess most people will have a view of what reasonable use is, it probably varies quite a lot between, people with different views on whatever the,

subject of that particular process is. We think that, the regu- on the regulatory relief, there's a, a potential missed opportunity to create a much more positive conversation about how landowners may be in effect recognized for the contribution they make to public outcomes.

Um, we already, and many councils I believe, do have a system of, rates relief around QEII covenanting. And, and our kind of view is perhaps if we can swap the conversation from being a council puts in some form of rule or restriction, and then there's some form of contest between the landowner and the council over what that means and what compensation should be provided.

If we flip it around and say, "Well, we'll identify some key values," and they might be biodiversity, might be natural landscape, they might be a range of things. If landowners use some form of voluntary protection that is in perpetuity, it could be two-- QEII covenant, but it wouldn't necessarily have to be, as long as it's enduring and legal, um-Then the council could consider their existing rates relief mechanisms to provide that recognition. And that, in our view, creates a positive conversation where landowners are taking...

And many of these, values only exist because landowners have protected them for the last 100, 150 years. Um, and it's not uncommon in our region to have landowners put significant chunks of their property into Q2 covenants, and then that's recognized via our rates rel- existing rates relief mechanism, which does two things. It means we're not spending a staggering amount of money on, the process of putting in protection.

It's a positive and constructive conversation with landowners, not a kinda contestable negative legal one. Uh, and hopefully it still delivers the outcome that, I guess, everybody's looking for, which is protecting those core environmental values. The last point we'd like to make is around collaboration, with our neighbors, Nelson City Council, and I understand there's a submission from Nelson City, around and requiring a compulsory working together, for spatial planning.

We're certainly open to the idea of working, collectively. We've done two, future development strategies collectively, under the Local Government Act, obviously not through the RMA or its replacement. But we're, we're not, convinced that making that mandatory is the right thing to do. We also are unclear exactly what working collaboratively means under the legislation. Does it result in a collaborative process that still ends up with two separate plans across regions or more than two if there's multiple regions collaborating, or does it result in one

single plan? Um, so yeah, like I say, we've had constructive, conversations with Nelson City. We're positively inclined towards walking with-- working with them on spatial planning specifically, but we don't believe it needs to be made mandatory. So in terms of recommendations, time frames just need consideration around the link between national direction and, national direction and limit setting alongside our planning processes, providing the ability to, a wider range of, relief, for landowners or property owners around regulations impacts, hopefully a more positive and constructive

one. Provide more explicit, explicit fees, charges, and cost recovery precisions, provisions, including single well-defined treaty clauses. Combine the bills into one piece of legislation and retain the option to have a joint regional spatial plan, but not make it mandatory. Open for questions.

Yeah. Good morn- great. Thank you. Well, the first question we'll have up, Rachel. Thank you. Thank, thanks, thanks, Tim, for that. Um, really good points you're making there. Uh, I'm just interested in this last comment about not being mandatory to have to work- Mm-hmm... with Nelson on the plans we had. You'll be pleased to hear very sensibly. So I mean, our, our, our kind of view is twofold. One is, making it mandatory. We've been through two FDS, as I said. Uh, we were concerned that quite a lot of the workload ended up falling on us. Uh, and if it's made mandatory, there's kinda no option.

You just have... Like, you kinda fall into the thing, you have to do it. If there's a conversation about it, that means we can... And I've had this conversation with my neighboring mayor about ensuring that the costs are split evenly, the staff contribution is split evenly, et cetera. Um, and also whether it's spatial planning, spatial planning is relatively straightforward, like I say.

But the FDS, we've done it collectively. We're one urban area on the MPS, urban development anyway. But when you come to the next lot of plans, then we're very different regions. They're a small, you know, small city, we're a vast rural area. And so our concern was also that if it involves both steps of planning, it may well be that we do spatial planning collectively, but actually the subsequent plans around environment and land use may be done separately. So we just wanna understand what the, the process is and still have the choice to go into

it. Like I say, I, I personally think it's the right thing to do. I'm just not convinced it needs to be mandatory in the legislation. Okay. Because it would be for other areas, you know, like Otago, for instance, is very... Queenstown's very different from Dunedin. Yeah. Um, but, but the cost thing, if the, if the legislation was clear around costs, would that make a difference to your, to your thoughts around it being mandatory or not?

Yeah. I mean, if it specifies that it has to be the, the way the cost is shared is fifty-fifty or thirty-third, thirty-three, thirty-three if it's three or whatever, yeah, that, that helps. But it's not just the cost, it's the actual... The burden of a large rural area trying to do the consultation, do all the work, actually, by definition, is different. So.

Okay. Okay, great. Thank you. Thanks for that, Tim. Uh, Lan. Tena koutou, thanks for your submission. Um, I wanted to pick up on your very clear comments that there doesn't actually seem to be anything in the bill that actually, for example, maintains water quality where it might be significantly, of a better quality than the environmental limits actually set.

What are the risks for Tasman? Like, is this a race to the bottom? W-what-- Why did you point that out? Yeah. Well, I think we are in a position where our existing water quality is very high, by na-national comparisons. Us and the West Coast are probably two areas with really high water quality. So, um-The, the risk is that if the limit is set below that, then there is the possibility, remote though it is likely to be, given we have good water quality because it's been maintained through all sorts of different, resource management

legislation over time. Um, the risk is there's the opportunity for that to be degraded through some form of, action, consent process, et cetera. So, I mean, we're just highlighting the fact that maintaining water quality where it's already really good is, for us, really important. Um, and you don't wanna- Thank you... pervert the outcome baked into the legislation.

Thank you. And can I ask another question, Chair? Yeah, yeah, fire away. Yep. Thank you. Um, the other aspect that I wanted to ask about was what seems like this very high bar when you are actually setting, your regional plans, and that's where you can't actually use regul- regulatory rules, unless the council is satisfied that voluntary measures, for example, will meet the, essentially the actions that you're trying to achieve in your action plan. H- how does Tasman perceive that?

Like, are you gonna be able to do this sort of big assessment, and m- mean, that means you're satisfied that the voluntary measures won't meet the action plans, and then you could put in regulatory rules? Can you help step us through what that would look like for you? I mean, I guess this cuts across the conversation that we had and the point we made around regulatory relief, is ultimately the best outcome and the most cost-efficient outcome is that people do the things we want them to do without rules and regulations.

That's the ideal outcome. And in my experience, probably 85-plus percent of people do do the right thing, and the regulatory kind of, thing is there to capture the people who, for whatever reason, you know, choose not to, to do things the right way. So look, I would like to think that if the legislation's clear enough and the tools are available, for councils to satisfy themselves that, certain things can be achieved voluntarily, that, you know, we don't need to have as heavy regulatory, role as perhaps we have had

in the past. But at the end of the day, I, I, I certainly firmly believe in our council's position is we've had a massive emphasis on working voluntarily, working with landowners. Probably our best example is our dairy, shed compliance program, where, yes, there are background rules and regulations, but the education, information sharing, and working with communities has actually provided us much better outcomes at much less cost.

Uh, and I'll have a follow-up question there actually. Just on the regulatory relief, 'cause I suppose where the, issue arises is where you get a situation whereby, the council, imposes a, a mand- a, ex- outstanding natural, outstanding natural landscape- Yeah... or SNA rules, et cetera, right?

And the landowner feels that actually that's gonna sim- significantly impact on the value of their property/ability to earn an income. Yeah. It's c- it's very easy. I, I, I applaud your approach, by the way. Um, it's very easy for, councils or whoever to say, "Well, this is what we want," but they don't carry the cost.

Whereas if there's a cost involved, they'll actually say, "Is this important to the wider community?" It's so important that they're prepared to help pay for it. That's the question. What's your thoughts? I'm not disagreeing with that. I actually think we just need to flip the conversation. Like, if you look all around the world, there are a vast number of countries where having some form of protection over your land is seen as an increase in value. You go to the UK to try and buy inside a national park or an area of outstanding natural beauty, you will pay a premium to live there because of

the protection. We've never managed to get our country to that space 'cause we do always see it as a negative and a negative impact. And I just think if we can flip that whole conversation and landowners go, "Actually, there's a financial upside in terms of potentially rates relief or some other form of relief, and my property value is seen as more valuable protected than not," then we all spend less money on legislation, regulation, rules, policies, and plans because actually landowners aren't contesting the need to protect.

They're actually going, "Actually, we can see it, and it's in our own hands. We've been given the opportunity to protect it." Council or government's role might be to identify the values and then say, "Here's a pathway for protection. It's in your hands. You can do it through this mechanism." QE2 is an example.

Yeah. Um, and then there is some relief. The backstop would be, if you don't do any of those things and you just say, "Actually, we don't wanna protect it at all," then there's always the regulatory backstop. But that should be the last cab off the rank, not the first, which is what it seems to be the- currently. Yeah. Uh, point taken. Thank you. Look, unfortunately, we've met time, but thanks for your presentation this morning, team. Really appreciate it.

You've made some great points, and, have a great day. Cheers. Cheers. Thank you very much for the opportunity. Cheers. Right. Our next submitter should be Spark. Whoops. One New Zealand or group of... Who have we got here? Um, good morning. Um, so you've got Graeme McCarrison from Spark.

Yes. And joining me is Fiona Matthews, from Connexa- Yeah... who, build our pole networks, and then Ross Langford, who represents Forty South and One New Zealand. Okay. Look, good morning, I'm Grant McCallum, the chair of the subcommittee. Um, you've got 15 minutes of our time.

Uh, we please take your submissions as read, but feel free to highlight a few points, and, we'll... And please leave some time for questions. Fire away. Thank you Absolutely. Um, and we were listening in on the TCF submission yesterday, so probably- Yeah... got some further detail that we'll add into this, this morning, so. So massive thank you for everyone who's working on the bills. Um, yeah, it's, it's a huge effort and I know there's lots of work going on-... in the background at the moment.

But, we represent critical networks that traverse New Zealand, connecting us, all of us, and internationally through our submarine cables. We depend on effective national direction. Currently, that's the National Environmental Standard, 2016, which is in the process of getting amended. And then inconsistent provisions for telecommunications within regional and district plans that make up the shortcomings of the existing national direction. So, detailed submission and edits are on page, start on page 10 of our submission. They're, kind of, highlighted.

We're very telco infrastructure focused. Um- Yep... so just stuck to our knitting, basically. Um, so for us, the first national instruments are critical. Um, and so we've asked for, for us to be involved as experts because we know the networks to work aside other key stakeholders, and that's local government, environmental groups, MFA, so forth. Uh, we suggest that the timeframe for developing the instruments, particularly the standards, needs to be extended. We're fully

aware of our NES amendments are 18 months and we still don't know what they're gonna look like. That's close, we hope before June. But it's just a good example. And then we also don't believe that dropping the existing RMA directions into the new framework is going to work, and so there's gonna need to be, hence, involvement of us and time to do that. And so as I said before, if for infrastructure telecommunications, we're looking to be comprehensively managed at, under national policy and national standards, and not have to

rely on bespoke provisions in the 17 combined plans, which then expands out into 67 land use plans. We just don't have the capacity or time to be simultaneously involved in the natural environment plans, land use plans, which are gonna be all notified by the end of, 2029 at the moment. Our other one is, permitted activities. We, and this has been well traversed, but yeah, we request the mandatory requirement for registration to

be removed, or at least for the first national direction standards for infrastructure to guide where registrations are necessary. And I suppose the example for us is that we undertake maintenance or upgrade our sites or build new sites primarily as permitted activities at the moment. So if someone phones up with a complaint about, a network issue or noise or something, at the moment, we're able to dispatch a technician out there to fix the problem very quickly. Or our program

of replacing antennas and upgrading network, these are all done under permitted activities. So at the moment, we're worried about if we're having to get thousands of consents just to work on our network, what that means for the public. Feels like a regulatory overreach for which we don't understand the problem, 'cause we don't generate compliance issues currently. But what it will do is generate a significant cost and delay to us being able to work on our networks and keep people connected. So we don't see the benefit, we just see it as a

huge cost. Could be tens of millions of dollars of delay, and I suspect there's other groups like that. Farmers probably, worrying about whether they can plow their fields anymore. Um, so there's that. Uh, we're also aware that the infrastructure goal needs to be slightly tweaked to basically strengthen it to balance it against the other, goals within both bills. And then having a comprehensive infrastructure definition would be useful.

And the other issue for us is we have a significant number of designations that are a mixture of buildings, exchanges, but cell sites also, often in sensitive areas. And so we've highlighted a number of changes. There's, some ambiguity between which rule trumps which, designation versus national standards. It's an issue under the RMA that no one's wanted to fix, so we've just, we've recommended a fix. The strategic need test feels onerous, where you have to have network in a particular location. And then just clarification around

construction plans. And we've suggested a third party, rule to enable, like for us, for co-location. So if, for example, for us, Two Degrees isn't a requiring authority, but if they wanted to go on a Spark pole, Connexa pole, and it was within the designation, then that should be enabled without, having to go through that.

So it's kind of that intent. So open for questions Thank you. I'll, I'll, I'll just kick it off. On that last point, I'm just... But- For Spark only... for Spark only. Okay, and, another question, infrastructure, the definition of infrastructure. Do you feel that, what... Um, the new national inf- national stan- direction of infrastructure has a definition and it, it just feels like dropping that in would be a, a useful thing.
